imprints.name: Whittlesey House
imprints.notes: Trade-books imprint, named for James H. McGraw's father-in-law. Phased out as McGraw-Hill exited trade publishing in the late 1960s.

publishers.notes: U.S. educational, professional, and (briefly) trade publisher. Founded 1909 by merger of McGraw Publishing and Hill Publishing.

imprints.name: Aldus Books
imprints.notes: London-based illustrated-books imprint.

imprints.name: Anchor Books
imprints.notes: Trade-paperback imprint founded by Jason Epstein; widely credited with creating the U.S. quality-paperback market.

imprints.name: Dolphin Books
imprints.notes: Mass-market paperback imprint.

imprints.name: Crime Club
imprints.notes: Mystery / detective fiction imprint and subscription book club.

publishers.notes: Founded by Frank Nelson Doubleday. Operated as Doubleday, Page (1900-1927), Doubleday, Doran (1927-1946) after merging with George H. Doran Company, then Doubleday & Company. Acquired by Bertelsmann in 1986 and folded into the Knopf Doubleday Publishing Group under Penguin Random House.

aliases: D. C. Heath and Company, a division of Raytheon Company

imprints.name: Lexington Books
imprints.notes: Academic / professional imprint. Now a standalone imprint of Rowman & Littlefield (out of scope here).

publishers.notes: U.S. educational publisher founded by Daniel Collamore Heath. Acquired by Raytheon in 1966 and operated as a division until 1995, when it was sold to Houghton Mifflin and absorbed.

imprints.name: Clarendon Press
imprints.notes: Imprint used historically for OUP's scholarly monographs. Named for Edward Hyde, Earl of Clarendon. By the late 20th century, used selectively; many works once Clarendon-imprinted appear simply as OUP.

publishers.notes: Department of the University of Oxford. Operates worldwide branches; the U.S. branch publishes much of the academic output relevant to mid-20th-century registrations.

imprints.name: Modern Library
imprints.notes: Cofounded by Bennett Cerf and Donald Klopfer in 1925 (acquired from Boni & Liveright). The Random House parent company was founded in 1927 specifically to expand around Modern Library.

imprints.name: Vintage Books
imprints.notes: Quality trade-paperback imprint, originally Knopf's, transferred to Random House proper after the 1960 Knopf acquisition.

imprints.name: Pantheon Books
imprints.notes: Independent until acquired by Random House in 1961. Notable for European and translated literature.

imprints.name: Alfred A. Knopf
imprints.notes: Acquired in 1960 but operated semi-autonomously with its own editorial identity. See its own canonical entry.

publishers.notes: Founded 1927 by Bennett Cerf and Donald Klopfer. Acquired by RCA in 1965, by Advance Publications in 1980, by Bertelsmann in 1998; in 2013 merged with Penguin to form Penguin Random House.

imprints.name: Borzoi Books
imprints.notes: House identifier rather than an editorially distinct imprint; the running borzoi (Russian wolfhound) colophon appears on most Knopf titles.

publishers.notes: Founded by Alfred A. Knopf Sr. and Blanche Knopf. Acquired by Random House in 1960 but retained editorial autonomy under the Knopf imprint name to the present day.

imprints.name: Free Press
imprints.notes: Founded by Jeremiah Kaplan as an academic / nonfiction imprint; acquired by Macmillan in 1960.

imprints.name: Collier Books
imprints.notes: Trade-paperback imprint launched after Macmillan acquired Crowell-Collier in 1960.

publishers.notes: U.S. arm of the British Macmillan Publishers, operationally independent from 1896. Sold by the family to U.S. owners in the early 20th century; later passed through several hands (Crowell-Collier 1960, Mortimer Zuckerman 1986, Robert Maxwell 1988) before becoming part of Holtzbrinck in 1994.

imprints.name: Harper Torchbooks
imprints.notes: Quality trade-paperback academic imprint.

imprints.name: Perennial Library
imprints.notes: Paperback reissue imprint.

publishers.notes: Founded as J. & J. Harper in 1817; Harper & Brothers from 1833. Merged with Row, Peterson and Company in 1962 to become Harper & Row. Acquired by News Corp in 1987 and merged with William Collins in 1989 to form HarperCollins.
Founded in 1846 (as Baker & Scribner). Operated independently for our entire era. Acquired by Macmillan in 1984; subsequently passed to Simon & Schuster in 1994.

imprints.name: Riverside Press
imprints.notes: In-house printing operation in Cambridge, Massachusetts; sometimes cited as the imprint of record on early-20th-century scholarly editions.

publishers.notes: Founded 1880 as Houghton, Mifflin and Company. Acquired D. C. Heath in 1995. Now part of Houghton Mifflin Harcourt.

imprints.name: Atlantic Monthly Press
imprints.notes: Joint imprint with The Atlantic Monthly. Books carried both colophons during the partnership.

publishers.notes: Founded 1837 in Boston. Acquired by Time Inc. in 1968, later Time Warner; now part of Hachette Book Group.

imprints.name: Studio Books
imprints.notes: Illustrated and art-book imprint, originally a joint venture with The Studio of London.

imprints.name: Compass Books
imprints.notes: Quality-paperback imprint.

publishers.notes: Founded by Harold Guinzburg and George Oppenheimer in 1925. Merged with Penguin in 1975 to form Viking Penguin; continues as an imprint within Penguin Random House.

Founded 1866 as Leypoldt & Holt. Merged with Rinehart and Company and the John C. Winston Company in 1960 to form Holt, Rinehart and Winston. Acquired by CBS in 1967; sold to Holtzbrinck in 1985, which restored the Henry Holt and Company name in 1985 for the trade arm.

imprints.name: Coward-McCann
imprints.notes: Acquired by Putnam in 1936; in 1971 merged with another Putnam imprint to become Coward, McCann & Geoghegan.

imprints.name: Coward, McCann & Geoghegan
imprints.notes: Successor to Coward-McCann.

imprints.name: Berkley Books
imprints.notes: Mass-market paperback imprint, acquired by Putnam in 1965.

publishers.notes: Founded 1838 by George Palmer Putnam. Sold to MCA in 1975; to Penguin Group in 1996. Today an imprint of Penguin Random House.

imprints.name: Harvest Books
imprints.notes: Quality-paperback imprint.

publishers.notes: Founded 1919 by Alfred Harcourt, Donald Brace, and (briefly) Will D. Howe as Harcourt, Brace and Howe. Renamed Harcourt, Brace & World after merging with World Book in 1960; Harcourt Brace Jovanovich after a 1970 acquisition. Acquired by Reed Elsevier in 2001, then by Houghton Mifflin in 2007.

imprints.name: Wiley-Interscience
imprints.notes: Scientific / technical journals and monographs imprint. Originated when Wiley acquired Interscience Publishers.

imprints.name: Halsted Press
imprints.notes: Joint imprint with Israel Universities Press / Israel Program for Scientific Translations, for English translations of Eastern European and Russian scientific works.

publishers.notes: Oldest continuously operating publisher in the United States. Founded by Charles Wiley in New York.

imprints.name: Liveright Publishing
imprints.notes: Acquired by Norton in 1974; originally Boni & Liveright (1917-1933). Carries the historical Liveright back-catalogue.

publishers.notes: Founded 1923 by William Warder Norton and Mary Dows Herter Norton as the publishing arm of the People's Institute lecture series. Employee-owned since the 1950s.

Founded 1836 by Joshua B. Lippincott in Philadelphia. Trade publishing operations acquired by Harper & Row in 1978 (becoming Harper & Row's Lippincott imprint); medical / professional Lippincott persists separately under Wolters Kluwer.

imprints.name: Pocket Books
imprints.notes: Mass-market paperback imprint that pioneered the U.S. paperback format in 1939. Initially a sister company; merged into Simon & Schuster in 1944 (sold to Field Enterprises 1957; reacquired 1966).

imprints.name: Washington Square Press
imprints.notes: Paperback imprint, originally for educational and classics titles; later trade.

imprints.name: Touchstone Books
imprints.notes: Trade-paperback imprint.

publishers.notes: Founded 1924 by Richard L. Simon and M. Lincoln Schuster. Acquired by Gulf+Western in 1975, became part of Paramount/Viacom, now an independent entity owned by KKR.

Founded 1834. Merged with P. F. Collier and Son to form Crowell-Collier Publishing Company in 1939; later Crowell, Collier and Macmillan after the 1960 Macmillan acquisition. The Crowell name persisted on trade titles until folded into Harper & Row in 1980.

Founded 1850 as Hood & Noble; became Bowen-Merrill in 1885 and Bobbs-Merrill in 1903. Indianapolis-based trade publisher. Acquired by Howard W. Sams in 1958, by ITT in 1966, sold to Macmillan in 1985 and dissolved.

Founded 1877 by Isaac Kaufmann Funk and Adam Willis Wagnalls. Best known for reference works (Standard Dictionary, New Standard Encyclopedia). Trade book operations folded after a series of ownership changes; reference brand continued under various owners.

Formed 1933 by merger of D. Appleton & Company (1825-1933) and The Century Company (1881-1933). Became Appleton-Century-Crofts in 1948 after acquiring F. S. Crofts & Co. Acquired by Meredith Corporation in 1960, then Prentice-Hall in 1973.

imprints.name: Hill and Wang
imprints.notes: Founded 1956 by Lawrence Hill and Arthur Wang. Acquired by Farrar, Straus and Giroux in 1971; continues as an FSG imprint.

imprints.name: Noonday Press
imprints.notes: Paperback imprint, acquired by Farrar, Straus in 1960.

imprints.name: Sunburst Books
imprints.notes: Children's-paperback imprint.

publishers.notes: Founded 1946 as Farrar, Straus & Company. Renamed multiple times as partners changed (Farrar, Straus & Young 1950, Farrar, Straus and Cudahy 1953, Farrar, Straus and Giroux 1964 when Roger W. Straus Jr. made Robert Giroux a partner). Acquired by Holtzbrinck in 1994; remains editorially independent.

Founded 1959 by Alfred A. Knopf Jr., Simon Michael Bessie, and Hiram Haydn after each left his prior publisher. Acquired by Scribner in 1978; Scribner+Atheneum acquired by Macmillan in 1984; Atheneum name retained as a children's imprint under Simon & Schuster after the 1994 Macmillan dissolution.

Founded 1942 in New York by Kurt and Helen Wolff and Jacques Schiffrin, all European-emigré publishers. Operated independently until 1961, when it was acquired by Random House. Editorially distinct under Random House and now Penguin Random House; cross-listed here for the pre-1961 independent era.
British publisher founded 1929 from the reorganization of Faber and Gwyer (1925-1929). T. S. Eliot was a director from 1925 until his death in 1965. Remains independent.

British publisher founded 1921 by Jonathan Cape and Wren Howard. Operated U.S. arm Cape & Smith (1929-1932) jointly with Harrison Smith. Merged with Chatto & Windus in 1969; acquired by Random House UK in 1987. Remains an imprint of Penguin Random House UK.

imprints.name: Pelican Books
imprints.notes: Nonfiction / educational paperback imprint, originally for popular science and history.

imprints.name: Puffin Books
imprints.notes: Children's-paperback imprint.

imprints.name: Penguin Classics
imprints.notes: Translated and classical-literature imprint.

imprints.name: Allen Lane
imprints.notes: Hardcover non-fiction imprint, named for Penguin's founder.

publishers.notes: Founded 1935 by Allen Lane as a paperback imprint of The Bodley Head; spun off as Penguin Books Ltd in 1936. Acquired by Pearson Longman in 1970; Penguin acquired Viking Press in 1975; merged with Random House in 2013 to form Penguin Random House.
British trade publisher founded 1931 by Jamie Hamilton ("Hamish" is the Gaelic form). Acquired by Penguin in 1986; remains an imprint of Penguin Random House UK.

imprints.name: Spectrum Books
imprints.notes: Quality-paperback nonfiction imprint.

publishers.notes: U.S. educational publisher founded 1913 by Charles Gerstenberg and Richard Ettinger. Acquired Appleton-Century-Crofts in 1973. Acquired by Gulf+Western in 1984, sold to Pearson in 1998; now Pearson Education's main trade educational imprint.
U.S. technical and scientific publisher. Acquired by Litton Industries in 1968 and merged with Van Nostrand to form Van Nostrand Reinhold.
Founded 1839 as Taylor & Dodd; renamed Dodd, Mead in 1870. Long-running U.S. trade and reference publisher. Closed in 1990 after sale to a holding company; back-catalogue dispersed.
Founded 1864 in Boston by Edward Payson Dutton; moved to New York 1869. Acquired by Elsevier in 1975, by John Dyson in 1979, by Penguin in 1986; now a Penguin Random House imprint.

aliases: New American Library of World Literature

imprints.name: Signet Books
imprints.notes: Mass-market paperback fiction imprint.

imprints.name: Mentor Books
imprints.notes: Mass-market paperback nonfiction imprint, oriented toward classics and serious nonfiction.

imprints.name: Plume
imprints.notes: Trade-paperback imprint.

publishers.notes: Spun off in 1948 from Penguin Books' U.S. arm by Kurt Enoch and Victor Weybright. Acquired by the Times Mirror Company in 1960, then Pearson in 1986; now part of Penguin Random House.
Founded 1933 by Nat Wartels and Robert Simon as a remainder house. Grew into trade publishing; acquired by Random House in 1988. Now the Crown Publishing Group within Penguin Random House.

British trade publisher founded 1890 by William Heinemann. Acquired by Doubleday in 1920, sold to Tilling Group in 1961, then Octopus Publishing Group in 1985; now an imprint of Penguin Random House UK.

British publisher founded 1889 by Algernon Methuen. Notable for early children's and academic titles. Acquired by Associated Book Publishers in 1968; now operates as Methuen Publishing Ltd, owned by Peter Tummons since 1997.

British publisher founded 1914 when Stanley Unwin acquired George Allen & Co. Notable for publishing Tolkien, Bertrand Russell, and many translations. Merged with Bell & Hyman in 1990 to form Unwin Hyman.

British trade publisher; the Eyre & Spottiswoode name had been the King's Printer firm since the early 19th century, but trade publishing was added in 1929. Merged with Methuen in 1968, then dissolved as a trade imprint by 1990.
British publisher founded 1848 by John Cassell. Acquired by Crowell-Collier in 1958; sold to CBS in 1973, to Macmillan in 1983, then to Orion Publishing Group in 1998; now an imprint of Hachette UK.

Founded 1917 by Albert Boni and Horace Liveright in New York. Boni left in 1918 (to found Albert & Charles Boni). Renamed Horace Liveright, Inc. in 1928 after Liveright bought out Boni's remaining interest; later Liveright Publishing Corporation. Bankrupted 1933; back-catalogue and the Liveright name eventually came to W. W. Norton in 1974. The original company famously launched Modern Library (1917; sold to Bennett Cerf and Donald Klopfer in 1925).

publishers.canonical: The Newcomen Society in North America
aliases: Newcomen Society of the United States
publishers.notes: American branch (founded 1923) of the British Newcomen Society for the Study of the History of Engineering and Technology. Published a series of short monographs based on members' addresses, typically about individual companies or industrialists. Many vault matches are sponsored-address monographs.
U.S. trade publisher founded 1926 with a Charles Garland endowment; published Saul Bellow, Joyce Carol Oates, and Dr. Seuss's first books. Acquired by Random House in 1988; folded in 1991.

British publisher founded 1887 by John Lane and Elkin Mathews. Allen Lane took over from his uncle John Lane in 1925; Bodley Head was Penguin's parent at Penguin's 1935 launch. Merged into Cape Goliard, then Random House UK; now a Penguin Random House UK imprint.

Formed 1947 by merger of George Routledge & Sons (founded 1836) and Kegan Paul, Trench, Trubner & Co. (founded 1877). Strong in academic and social-science titles. Merged into Routledge as part of the Taylor & Francis Group in 1985; the Routledge brand continues.

Founded 1947 by Jeremiah Kaplan in Glencoe, Illinois (as The Free Press of Glencoe) for academic-nonfiction. Acquired by Macmillan in 1960; eventually became a Simon & Schuster imprint after the 1994 Macmillan dissolution. Cross-listed here for the pre-1960 independent era.
